mapQuestions.question: Which continent is the USA on?
mapQuestions.answer: North America. The USA sits between Canada to the north and Mexico to the south 🌎

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of the USA?
mapQuestions.answer: Washington, D.C. — not Washington state, which is on the opposite side of the country. D.C. stands for "District of Columbia", and it is not part of any state.

mapQuestions.question: How many states are there?
mapQuestions.answer: 50. The most recent two (Alaska and Hawaii) joined in 1959. That is why the flag has 50 stars (one per state) and 13 stripes (one per original colony).

mapQuestions.question: What is the largest state by area?
mapQuestions.answer: Alaska, by a long way. Alaska is bigger than Texas, California and Montana combined. But more people live in California than in any other state.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in the USA?
mapQuestions.answer: Denali in Alaska, 6,190 metres. Its name means "the tall one" in the Athabaskan language of the local people.

foods.name: Cheeseburger
foods.description: A beef patty in a soft bun with American cheese, lettuce, tomato, pickle, onion and ketchup. The American national meal, often eaten with fries and a soda.

foods.name: Hot dog
foods.description: A grilled or boiled sausage in a long soft bun, with ketchup, mustard, onions and relish. Eaten at baseball games, on city streets from carts, and at backyard barbecues.

foods.name: Diner pancakes
foods.description: A tall stack of fluffy pancakes drenched in maple syrup, with crispy bacon and an egg on the side. Served at "diner" restaurants that look the same as they did in the 1950s.

foods.name: Pizza (NY-style or Chicago deep-dish)
foods.description: In New York, huge thin floppy slices folded in half to eat on the go. In Chicago, a deep-pan pie that takes 30 minutes to bake. Both started with Italian immigrants.

foods.name: BBQ ribs and brisket
foods.description: Slow-cooked meat smoked over wood for hours, glazed with sticky sauce. Texas, Tennessee, Kansas City and the Carolinas all argue about who does it best.

foods.name: Apple pie
foods.description: A double-crust pie filled with cinnamon-sugar apples, served warm with a scoop of vanilla ice cream on top ("à la mode"). "As American as apple pie".

scavengerItems.label: An American flag flying outside a normal house
A big red fire hydrant on a street corner
A bald eagle (on a flag, sign, or in the wild)
A diner with red booths, a long counter and a chrome milkshake machine
A huge pickup truck used as everyday transport
A giant tree (sequoia, redwood) or a national park sign
A football (the American kind, oval and pointed) being thrown
A "drive-thru" coffee shop, bank or pharmacy
Someone wearing cowboy boots or a real cowboy hat in everyday life
An old red barn with a faded painted sign on it
A Native American memorial, museum or road sign

geoMatches.description: On the east coast. Skyscrapers, the Statue of Liberty, yellow cabs, 24-hour pizza, and people from every country on Earth speaking 800+ languages.
In Arizona. A canyon 446 km long, 29 km wide and over 1.5 km deep, carved by the Colorado River over millions of years. Bigger than you can imagine.
On the west coast. Hollywood, Disneyland, the giant sequoias of Yosemite, Silicon Valley, surf beaches, the Golden Gate Bridge in San Francisco.
In Wyoming. The world's first national park, sitting on a giant ancient supervolcano. Hot springs, geysers (Old Faithful), and herds of wild bison.
In Louisiana. The birthplace of jazz, French-influenced food (gumbo, beignets), and the wild Mardi Gras carnival every February.
The peninsula sticking out into the Gulf and the Atlantic. Walt Disney World, the Everglades swamps full of alligators, Cape Canaveral rocket launches.

senses.smell: Diner bacon and coffee at 6am, hot dog carts on a city street, salty Pacific breeze, sagebrush in the desert, popcorn at a baseball game, freshly cut grass at a high-school football field…
senses.hear: "Hey, y'all", police sirens, train horns in the night, country music in a Texas diner, baseball commentary, a marching band, the constant hum of huge highways…
senses.taste: Crispy salty bacon, sweet maple syrup on pancakes, melted American cheese, ice-cold root beer, ketchup on everything, free refills on every drink…
senses.feel: Cold A/C in summer, scorching car seats baking in the sun, soft grass under bare feet, sticky humidity in the south, the freeze of a 7-Eleven slushie on a hot day…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why does the USA have a giant green statue of a woman?
stories.body: In 1886, France gave the United States a giant copper statue of a robed woman holding a torch, as a gift to celebrate 100 years since American independence. The Statue of Liberty stands 93 metres tall on a small island in New York Harbour. For over a hundred years, immigrants arriving by ship from Europe passed her as the first thing they saw of America. At her feet is a poem with the famous line "Give me your tired, your poor, your huddled masses yearning to breathe free." Around 12 million people passed through Ellis Island, the immigration station next to the statue, between 1892 and 1954. Almost half of all Americans alive today have an ancestor who came through there.

stories.question: Why does the American flag have 50 stars and 13 stripes?
stories.body: The 13 red and white stripes stand for the 13 original colonies that became the first states of the USA in 1776 (after fighting a war of independence against Britain). The 50 stars stand for the 50 states the country has today. Every time a new state joined the country, a new star was added to the flag. The most recent two — Alaska and Hawaii — joined in 1959, finally completing the 50. Many American schools say the "Pledge of Allegiance" to the flag every morning, with hands on their hearts. Flags fly outside houses, schools, fire stations, even on people's pickup trucks. Most countries do not have anything like it.

stories.question: Why does the ground in Yellowstone shoot steam?
stories.body: Yellowstone was the world's first national park, created in 1872. It sits on top of an ancient "supervolcano" that has erupted a few times over the last 2 million years and is still alive deep underground. The heat finds its way to the surface as geysers (jets of hot water and steam), boiling hot springs, and bubbling mud pots. The most famous geyser, Old Faithful, erupts a column of water around 40 metres into the air roughly every 90 minutes, just like clockwork. Yellowstone is also home to North America's last big herd of wild bison, giant shaggy beasts that once roamed in the millions before settlers nearly wiped them out. Park rules say stay at least 25 metres away, but tourists get gored almost every year by trying to take selfies with them.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A "supervolcano" sounds scary, but it has not erupted properly for 640,000 years. Should people still live near things like that?

stories.question: Why has America always been so divided about race?
stories.body: Before the United States existed, Spanish, French and English settlers colonised Native American land for centuries, displacing millions. From 1619 to 1865, Africans were brought across the Atlantic by force and enslaved on huge plantations. In 1861 eleven Southern states tried to break away from the rest of the country to keep slavery legal, sparking the American Civil War, the bloodiest war in US history, around 620,000 dead. The North won and slavery was abolished. But for the next hundred years, many Southern states passed "Jim Crow" laws keeping Black Americans out of white schools, restaurants, water fountains and buses. The Civil Rights Movement of the 1950s and 60s, led by people like Martin Luther King Jr. and Rosa Parks, fought non-violently to change those laws. The wounds of slavery and segregation still shape American politics, music, sports, food and culture today.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Bad things that happened a long time ago can still affect a country today. Can you think of an example from your own country?

stories.question: What makes America "American"?
stories.body: The USA is younger than many other countries — it only became independent in 1776. It was the first big country built on the idea that ordinary people should choose their own leaders with no king. The opening words of the Constitution are "We the People". The Declaration of Independence says "All men are created equal", though it took a long, long time for that idea to actually include women, Native Americans and Black Americans. Around 350 million people now live there, speaking more than 350 languages. America invented or first popularised jazz, blues, rock and roll, hip-hop, the modern movie industry, the personal computer, the internet, and most of the big tech companies in your pocket right now. It is also a country where 50 different states can pass very different laws, so life in rural Texas feels almost nothing like life in central New York.

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you have eaten in the USA so far?
Everything is so BIG here — cars, drinks, meals, buildings. What's been the biggest "wow that's huge" moment?
How friendly have strangers been to you here?
Have you spotted three different accents in one day? (Southern, New York, California…)
A flag is on almost every house. What do you make of that?
The USA has 350 million people and 50 states. What's one thing that feels totally different from where we live?

wordSearch.hint: American foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Eastern grey squirrel
animals.description: In every park, every backyard, every campus from coast to coast.

animals.name: White-tailed deer
animals.description: In suburban gardens, national parks, side of every interstate at dusk.

animals.name: Bald eagle
animals.description: America's national bird. Best spots: Alaska, the Pacific Northwest, the Mississippi River.

animals.name: Black bear
animals.description: In Yellowstone, the Smokies, Yosemite. Don't feed them, ever.

animals.name: American bison
animals.description: Yellowstone has the only continuously wild herd. Massive, shaggy, can run 50 km/h.

animals.name: American alligator
animals.description: In Florida's Everglades, on golf courses, anywhere with warm water.

animals.name: Grizzly bear
animals.description: In Yellowstone, Glacier National Park, Alaska. Carry bear spray.
